comment_text.iti109:12.4: The duplicated tattha tattha is not used in a limiting sense such as the English “here and there”, but in a distributive sense, “in each and every place”.

root_text.iti109:2.1: “Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, puriso nadiyā sotena ovuyheyya piyarūpasātarūpena.
root_text.iti109:2.2: Tamenaṁ cakkhumā puriso tīre ṭhito disvā evaṁ vadeyya:
root_text.iti109:2.3: ‘kiñcāpi kho tvaṁ, ambho purisa, nadiyā sotena ovuyhasi piyarūpasātarūpena, atthi cettha heṭṭhā rahado saūmi sāvaṭṭo sagaho sarakkhaso yaṁ tvaṁ, ambho purisa, rahadaṁ pāpuṇitvā maraṇaṁ vā nigacchasi maraṇamattaṁ vā dukkhan’ti.
root_text.iti109:2.4: Atha kho so, bhikkhave, puriso tassa purisassa saddaṁ sutvā hatthehi ca pādehi ca paṭisotaṁ vāyameyya.
root_text.iti109:3.1: Upamā kho me ayaṁ, bhikkhave, katā atthassa viññāpanāya.
root_text.iti109:4.1: ‘Piyarūpaṁ sātarūpan’ti kho, bhikkhave, channetaṁ ajjhattikānaṁ āyatanānaṁ adhivacanaṁ.
root_text.iti109:5.1: ‘Heṭṭhā rahado’ti kho, bhikkhave, pañcannaṁ orambhāgiyānaṁ saṁyojanānaṁ adhivacanaṁ.
root_text.iti109:10.1: ‘Hatthehi ca pādehi ca vāyāmo’ti kho, bhikkhave, vīriyārambhassetaṁ adhivacanaṁ.
root_text.iti109:11.1: ‘Cakkhumā puriso tīre ṭhito’ti kho, bhikkhave, tathāgatassetaṁ adhivacanaṁ arahato sammāsambuddhassā”ti.
root_text.iti109:13.1: Ayampi attho vutto bhagavatā, iti me sutanti.

translation_text.iti109:0.3: The Chapter on the Brahmin’s Offering of the Teaching
translation_text.iti109:1.1: This was said by the Buddha, the Perfected One: that is what I heard.
translation_text.iti109:2.1: “Suppose a person was being carried along by a river current that seemed nice and pleasant.
translation_text.iti109:2.2: If a person with clear eyes saw them, they’d say:
translation_text.iti109:2.3: ‘My friend, even though the river current carrying you along seems nice and pleasant, downstream there is a lake with waves and whirlpools, saltwater crocodiles and monsters. When you reach there it will result in death or deadly pain.’
translation_text.iti109:2.4: Then, when they heard what was said, they’d paddle against the stream using their hands and feet.
translation_text.iti109:3.1: I’ve made up this simile to make a point.
translation_text.iti109:3.3: ‘River current’ is a term for craving.
translation_text.iti109:4.1: ‘Seeming nice and pleasant’ is a term for the six interior sense fields.
translation_text.iti109:5.1: ‘A downstream lake’ is a term for the five lower fetters.
translation_text.iti109:6.1: ‘Danger of waves’ is a term for anger and distress.
translation_text.iti109:7.1: ‘Whirlpool’ is a term for the five kinds of sensual stimulation.
translation_text.iti109:8.1: ‘Saltwater crocodiles and monsters’ is a term for ladies.
translation_text.iti109:9.1: ‘Against the stream’ is a term for renunciation.
translation_text.iti109:10.1: ‘Paddling with hands and feet’ is a term for rousing energy.
translation_text.iti109:11.1: ‘A person with clear eyes’ is a term for the Realized One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha.”
translation_text.iti109:12.1: “In pain they’d give up sensual pleasures,
translation_text.iti109:12.2: aspiring to the future sanctuary from the yoke.
translation_text.iti109:12.3: With deep understanding and heart well freed,
translation_text.iti109:12.5: That knowledge master <j>who has completed the spiritual journey,
translation_text.iti109:12.6: and gone to the end of the world, <j>is called ‘one who has gone beyond’.”
translation_text.iti109:13.1: This too is a matter that was spoken by the Blessed One: that is what I heard.

variant_text.iti109:6.1: Ūmibhayan’ti kho → saūmīti kho (bj, sya-all); saummīti (pts-vp-pli1)
